subject: Earning two nobles on same turn

posts.body:
My wife earned a visit from two nobles after purchasing a card last night. I realize you can only obtain one noble at the end of a turn, so she only took one of the nobles at the end of that turn. Then, at the end of her next turn, she obtained the second noble (which she earned the previous turn).
Did we play this right? Or can you only receive one visit from a noble per game?

Smerdy643 wrote:

My wife earned a visit from two nobles after purchasing a card last night. I realize you can only obtain one noble at the end of a turn, so she only took one of the nobles at the end of that turn. Then, at the end of her next turn, she obtained the second noble (which she earned the previous turn).
Did we play this right? Or can you only receive one visit from a noble per game?

you played right

one per turn gives another player a shot at getting the other one
Oh, I didn't realize the other player still had a shot at the second noble. Thanks much. I don't think I could have obtained it anyway in our game last night

Yep, you did it right! In fact, considerations of which noble your opponent might get on the next turn can influence which one you take first. 
For example, assume you have 4 onyx, 3 sapphires, and 4 diamonds; the other player has 3 onyx, 3 sapphires, and 2 diamonds; and you're at the end of your turn. You'd likely want to take the noble attracted to 3 onyx, 3 sapphires, and 3 diamonds instead of the one attracted by 4 onyx and 4 diamonds. The first noble is one your opponent could attract by getting a diamond on the next turn; the second would be unattainable on a single turn.
Right, thanks. We never imagined one of us would get TWO nobles in one game and that we'd be facing this situation. We usually have trouble getting one! Maybe it means we're getting better ha

Smerdy643 wrote:

Right, thanks. We never imagined one of us would get TWO nobles in one game and that we'd be facing this situation. We usually have trouble getting one! Maybe it means we're getting better ha

Does sound like you are getting better. Of course, it depends on how the demands of the nobles line up.  In many games, especially 2p, getting two nobles would be quite difficult.

The last time my wife and I played, I was able to snag a top-tier card which completed a noble for an 8 point turn that ended the game. Maybe something like that is your next challenge. Have fun!

Pattersonclan wrote:

Does sound like you are getting better. Of course, it depends on how the demands of the nobles line up.  In many games, especially 2p, getting two nobles would be quite difficult.

I disagree. There are 3 nobles out with 2 players. At least the way I would play, in almost every game there is no noble left, so one of the players had to get 2. And in many cases that was the winner.

schloetterer wrote:

Pattersonclan wrote:

Does sound like you are getting better. Of course, it depends on how the demands of the nobles line up.  In many games, especially 2p, getting two nobles would be quite difficult.

I disagree. There are 3 nobles out with 2 players. At least the way I would play, in almost every game there is no noble left, so one of the players had to get 2. And in many cases that was the winner.

Then you haven't played the rush strategy
That strategy focused on sniping point cards with engine building as a side tool, and nobles in those game are pretty much powerless

In high level tournament play, the chance of a player having even one noble as the winner of a game goes *down* the higher the level of play.

The better the player, the lower the chance of using even one noble in a win strategy.

JoeBoardgame wrote:

In high level tournament play, the chance of a player having even one noble as the winner of a game goes *down* the higher the level of play.

The better the player, the lower the chance of using even one noble in a win strategy.

Do you know if the game designer thought about tweaking it a bit, like giving +1 point to each noble or whatever?

I think no
The game is about eficiently gaining point, and the rush strategy did just that
